The error-magnitude figure is also independently confirmed: the paper states the human "produced ... 15–29% more error than the model in the MBA student forecasting task of Studies 1, 2, and 4 and 90–97% more error than the model in the airline passenger forecasting task of Studies 3a and 3b" — combining to exactly the claim's "15-97% more error than the model depending on the study/domain" (and the discussion section separately restates this as "13-97% more error"). One caveat: the claim's supplied "Supporting quote" itself contains a transcription slip (it reads "Seeing the model perform significantly decreased confidence in the human's forecasts..." where the actual paper says "Seeing the human perform..."), but this is an error in the excerpt given to me, not in the underlying claim, which correctly attributes the effect to watching the human. A 2021 follow-up (Lin, Kim & Tong, SSRN 3891832) found the asymmetry reverses in repeated real-world settings (insulin dosing), but this is a boundary-condition finding about generalizability, not a refutation of what Dietvorst et al.'s original four studies found — the claim is scoped to that paper's studies specifically.

confirmed.claim: The trust-restoring effect of control is driven by the mere presence of some agency, not by the size of the discretion granted — a tiny modification allowance works nearly as well as a larger one.
confirmed.quote: participants' preference for modifiable algorithms was relatively insensitive to the magnitude of the modifications they were able to make
confirmed.evidence: Verified against the primary source (Dietvorst, Simmons & Massey 2018, Management Science 64(3):1155-1170, fetched full PDF of Studies 1-2). Study 2 directly tests the claim: participants were randomly assigned to adjust-by-10, adjust-by-5, or adjust-by-2 percentile conditions vs. a can't-change control. Choice-to-use rates were 71%, 71%, and 68% respectively (vs 47% in can't-change) — a chi-square test across the three magnitude conditions was non-significant: χ²(2, N=623)=0.42, p=0.809. The paper's own discussion states: 'participants were relatively insensitive to the amount by which they could adjust the model's forecasts... the amount of control needed to obtain those benefits is actually quite small,' and explicitly frames this as evidence people want 'some' control, not 'greater' control. This is an exact match to the claim, not an overreach — the claim's phrasing ('tiny modification allowance works nearly as well as a larger one') is a direct, non-exaggerated paraphrase of the 68/71/71% finding. Source quality is high (top-tier peer-reviewed journal, well-cited, N=816 in Study 2, preregistered-style design with clear stats). Searched for contradicting evidence/replications: found a 2023 CHI follow-up (arXiv:2303.12896, 'Overcoming Algorithm Aversion: A Comparison between Process and Outcome Control') that reinforces rather than contradicts the theme — it finds 'some control' (of the right type) matters more than the extent of control. No credible source disputing the magnitude-insensitivity finding was found. Minor caveat: the claim's framing as 'trust-restoring' is an interpretive gloss — the original paper measures willingness-to-use/choice behavior and satisfaction rather than using the word 'trust' directly — but this is a standard and reasonable mapping in the algorithm-aversion literature, not a misrepresentation.

confirmed.claim: Interface interventions that force active interaction with the changed element (the 'Swipe' and 'Type' attractors) remain effective at directing attention even after many repeated exposures, unlike passive/attention-directing designs.
confirmed.quote: two attractors, Swipe and Type, remained effective even after many exposures.
confirmed.evidence: The quote is verbatim from the primary source (Bravo-Lillo et al., "Harder to Ignore? Revisiting Pop-Up Fatigue and Approaches to Prevent It," USENIX SOUPS 2014). Independent web searches surfacing paper summaries/citations converge on the same finding using near-identical language: "for the two attractors that forced the user to interact with the text field containing the change [Swipe and Type], increasing the level of habituation did not decrease the proportion of participants who responded to the change," in contrast to passive attractors and the control, where "increasing habituation reduced the proportion of participants who noticed the change." This directly supports both halves of the claim under review: (1) Swipe/Type remain effective across many exposures, and (2) this differs from passive/attention-directing designs, which degrade with habituation. A partial replication (Busse et al., "Do We Snooze If We Can't Lose?", USEC/NDSS 2019) failed to fully replicate the original study's broader results, but specifically corroborated Swipe and Type as "resistant to habituation" with "similar compliance trends" — i.e., the one part of the original study most relevant to this claim held up under independent replication, even though the paper's overall replication was imperfect. Source quality (peer-reviewed, well-cited primary HCI/security paper, authored by recognized researchers including Lorrie Cranor) is adequate for a claim of this specificity and strength — it is a bounded, well-scoped empirical finding, not an extraordinary or sweeping claim. No credible source found disputing the Swipe/Type-specific finding. Minor caveats: study is an artificial MTurk lab experiment (external validity to real-world agent UIs is inferential, not directly tested) and is now 11 years old, but neither undermines the narrow claim as stated, which is about that specific study's findings.

confirmed.claim: Increasing how often users are prompted to make a decision (asking on every visit vs. asking on only 25% of visits) significantly worsens long-term retention (hazard ratio 0.78, p<0.003), i.e., higher ask-frequency measurably drives attrition.
confirmed.quote: Asking users "on all visits" showed worse retention than asking "25% of visits" (hazard ratio 0.78, p<0.003)
confirmed.evidence: Verified directly against the primary source (arxiv 2101.11743, Kovacs/Wu/Bernstein, "Not Now, Ask Later," CHI 2021 — read pages 1-7 of the actual PDF, including Table 1 and Figure 7 in Section 5.2). This is a randomized controlled experiment on the HabitLab browser extension: users were randomly assigned to have a difficulty-preference prompt shown at 0%, 25%, 50%, or 100% of visits, and retention (time-to-uninstall/attrition, via survival/Cox proportional-hazards analysis) was measured. Table 1 shows exactly: reference = 100% of visits; 25% of visits → Hazard Ratio 0.78 (95% CI 0.66-0.92), p=0.003. This is an exact match to the supporting quote and the claim's stated numbers. The paper's own text states: 'Retention is significantly higher when the prompts are shown 25% of the time, compared to 100% of the time.' Source quality is high — peer-reviewed CHI (top HCI venue) paper with a genuine randomized experiment (not observational/correlational), N=1,108 observations, 1,029 events, reported with full CI/p-values/likelihood-ratio and Wald tests, so it comfortably meets the bar for this claim's strength. Not outdated (2021, and interruption/friction-retention dynamics in HCI aren't a fast-moving-model-capability topic where 5-year-old findings expire). Not marketing/press-release content. One nuance the claim's 'i.e.' gloss elides: the relationship isn't strictly monotonic — the 0%-of-visits (never asked) condition had HR=0.93 (p=0.47, not significant) versus the 100% reference, i.e., never-asking wasn't reliably better than asking-every-time either; only the specific 25% frequency significantly outperformed 100%. So the safer framing is 'occasional asking beats constant asking' (which is what the paper itself concludes) rather than a strict monotonic dose-response 'more asking always worse.' This is a precision quibble on the generalizing clause, not a refutation of the core quoted statistic, which is accurate and well-supported. I found no contradicting evidence in search results; independent search summaries of this paper corroborate the same HR/p-value (one paraphrase said 'p<0.005' vs the table's exact p=0.003 — a rounding artifact in a secondary summary, not a discrepancy in the primary source I read directly). One scope caveat worth flagging to the synthesis stage (not grounds for refuting factual accuracy): HabitLab is itself a personal behavior-tracking/digital-wellbeing tool, and the grounding says tracking-app abandonment/lapse typology was already settled by Cairn R3 and is out of scope for R8. However, this particular finding is a general interaction-friction-vs-retention causal experiment (sub-area 3: friction economics), not a lapse-typology study, so it's plausibly still in-scope — but the adjacency to the excluded domain is worth a note.

confirmed.claim: The authors conclude that excessive prompting increases attrition even though it only costs users a small amount of time per interaction (median 1.55 seconds to answer the prompt) — friction from decision frequency, not decision cost, is what drives abandonment.
confirmed.quote: median of 1.55 seconds
confirmed.evidence: Verified directly against the primary source (Kovacs, Wu, Bernstein, "Not Now, Ask Later," CHI 2021, arXiv:2101.11743) via ar5iv full text. The paper explicitly states "median of the distribution is 1.55 seconds" for time to answer a difficulty-preference prompt, and explicitly uses the term "attrition": "low-frequency prompting reduces attrition compared to high-frequency prompting" and "excessive prompting increasing attrition." Crucially, the authors separate time-cost from frequency: "there is no significant difference in the duration of sessions when a difficulty prompt is shown, vs not shown" (ruling out per-interaction time cost as the driver), while retention was "significantly higher when users are asked... at a low frequency (25% of visits)... compared to being asked on all visits" (p<0.005) — an effect the authors attribute to annoyance/interruption burden, backed by direct user feedback: "we received feedback from many users that they had uninstalled HabitLab because they were annoyed by excessive difficulty choice prompts." The claim's "decision frequency, not decision cost" framing is a paraphrase rather than a verbatim quote, but it is a faithful synthesis of the authors' own contrast (minimal per-prompt time cost vs. frequency-driven annoyance/uninstalls), not an overreach. Source quality is strong (peer-reviewed CHI paper, N=8,000+ users, 16,183 timed responses). One caveat for downstream use: this is a single case study of a self-directed browsing-nudge extension, not an AI-agent confirmation-prompt study, so it's directly on-topic for sub-area 3 (general interaction-friction economics) but should not be treated as agent-specific evidence.

confirmed.claim: Despite steadily weakening their actual behavior-change commitment, most users decline to permanently lock in the easier setting and instead keep opting to be re-asked to re-negotiate difficulty on their next visit — a 'not now, ask later' pattern of deferred re-commitment rather than one-time renegotiation.
confirmed.quote: many still expect to return to the harder interventions imminently: they repeatedly choose to be asked to change difficulty again on the next visit
confirmed.evidence: Verified via arxiv.org/abs/2101.11743. The paper's actual title IS the claim: "Not Now, Ask Later: Users Weaken Their Behavior Change Regimen Over Time, But Expect To Re-Strengthen It Imminently" by Kovacs, Wu, and Bernstein (CHI '21), based on log analysis of 8,000+ HabitLab users (a tool restricting excessive online browsing via configurable-difficulty interventions). The arxiv abstract confirms the mechanism precisely: users progressively shift to easier interventions over time, yet "users frequently opt to be prompted again on their next visit rather than permanently saving their preference for easier restrictions" — i.e., deferred re-commitment ("not now, ask later") rather than one-time renegotiation of the easier setting. This is not an overreach or misread; the claim is essentially a restatement of the paper's own title and central abstract finding, not an inference stretched from a supporting quote. The PDF's full text was not directly extractable (image-heavy encoding), so I could not pull the exact in-body sentence containing "many still expect to return to the harder interventions imminently," but the abstract-level evidence independently and strongly corroborates the claim's substance and the supporting quote's language. Source quality is high (peer-reviewed CHI paper, primary source, large-N behavioral log study) and well-matched to the claim's strength (a descriptive behavioral pattern, not an extraordinary causal claim). No contradicting evidence found. Not outdated — it's a stable behavioral/UX finding, not a claim about fast-moving model capabilities. Note: this HabitLab/digital-wellness domain overlaps with what the grounding flags as "personal-tracking abandonment ... already settled by Cairn R3, do not research" — the claim itself checks out factually, but its relevance to the general human-agent autonomy-boundary question (vs. tracking-app-specific churn) is a scope question for the parent synthesis to weigh, not a refutation ground.

confirmed.claim: Interrupting warnings that require a click-through action produce a dramatically larger behavioral effect than passive/inline warnings — in a controlled search-task experiment, click-through rate on the interstitial (interrupting) warning was much lower (users bypassed it far less) than on the contextual (passive) warning.
confirmed.quote: The CTR for the contextual warning was very high: 33/40... The CTR for the interstitial warning was much lower: 18/40.
confirmed.evidence: Verified against the actual paper (Kaiser et al., "Adapting Security Warnings to Counter Online Disinformation," arXiv:2008.10772, published at USENIX Security 2021, Princeton/Cornell authors). Fetched both the arxiv PDF/abstract and the ar5iv HTML rendering. The exact quote "CTR for the contextual warning was very high: 33/40... The CTR for the interstitial warning was much lower: 18/40" is verbatim in the paper's laboratory-study results section (n=20 per condition, simulated search task). CTR here means click-through past the warning (bypass rate), so the claim's interpretation — interstitial/interrupting warnings suppressed bypass far more than contextual/passive warnings (45% vs 82.5% click-through) — is a correct, non-overreaching reading of the data, and matches the paper's own abstract framing: "Users routinely ignore contextual warnings, but users notice interstitial warnings." A supplementary search on security-warning habituation literature turned up a consistent, independent finding in the same direction (active/interstitial warnings heeded by ~79% of participants vs. essentially 0% for a passive warning in a separate study), and no contradicting source. Source is peer-reviewed at a top venue, not outdated (2020-21 HCI/security-warning research remains valid, not a fast-moving-capabilities field), and not marketing content. One minor nuance not in the claim: the paper notes neither comprehension nor fear of harm moderated the effect (suggesting friction/annoyance may drive it rather than informed choice) — this qualifies the mechanism but does not contradict the stated numerical/behavioral claim.

refuted.claim: Automation complacency (poorer detection of automation failures than manual monitoring) reliably appears only under multiple-task load, where a competing manual task draws attention away from the automated task; under single-task conditions, monitoring is near-perfect.

Habituation to a repeated security dialog sharply degrades users' ability to notice a meaningful change in it: going from low to high exposure (1 rep vs 20 reps) produced roughly a three-fold drop in the proportion of participants who noticed/responded to the change, under the control condition.

The authors argue autonomy should be treated as a design decision independent of the agent's capability level -- a highly capable agent can still be deliberately kept at low autonomy (frequent consultation), and a less-capable agent can be run at high autonomy.

Anthropic replaced repeated per-action confirmation with an 'auto mode' backed by OS-level sandboxing (Seatbelt/bubblewrap), which reduced permission prompts by 84% while substituting deterministic environmental containment for human confirmation friction.

Horvitz explicitly ties the cost of wrongly acting (blast radius of an erroneous autonomous action) to the probability threshold required before autonomous action is permitted: increasing the cost of an unwanted action raises the threshold, i.e. higher-cost/harder-to-reverse actions require higher confidence before the system may act on its own rather than asking.

When an automated decision aid gives a confident but false recommendation, trained operators can follow it at very high (up to 100%) commission-error rates even when contradicting evidence is directly available on other displays and operators had been told to cross-check — showing human-in-the-loop review can fail as a safety backstop rather than catching the error.

System reliability calibration is non-monotonic: moderate (~70%) reliability produced better human monitoring/performance than higher-but-imperfect reliability, because near-perfect (but not perfect) automation induces complacency and erodes vigilance.
Human users continue to trust and use automation even with substantial error rates, up to roughly 30%, as long as errors are predictable.
Automation bias occurs simply because an automated system is present, driven by a general human tendency to default to the lowest-effort course of action rather than independently verify.
People often refuse to use algorithms after seeing them err even once, despite the algorithms outperforming human forecasters on average — this is the core algorithm aversion phenomenon.

Giving people even minimal ability to adjust an imperfect algorithm's output (a small modifiable margin) significantly increases their willingness to adopt and rely on it, and improves their actual forecasting performance versus using the unmodifiable algorithm or their own judgment alone.
